name: Waste Reduction Strategy
purpose: Translates the waste data collected in B057 into a systematic reduction strategy targeting the root causes of waste — over-production, poor stock rotation, preparation errors, and demand forecasting failures. This block provides the frameworks and actions needed to reduce waste percentage measurably and sustainably.

detailed_objectives: Analyse waste data to identify the top causes of avoidable waste
Implement production planning that matches preparation quantities to anticipated demand
Establish stock rotation and shelf-life management to prevent expiry waste
Reduce preparation errors through training, standardisation, and quality systems
Set measurable waste reduction targets and track progress monthly
secondary_objectives: Explore waste redistribution channels (Too Good To Go, local food banks, staff meals)
Optimise ordering quantities to prevent over-stocking of perishable items
Reduce environmental impact through measurable waste diversion from landfill
Build a cost-saving case that demonstrates the financial return on waste reduction efforts

why_this_matters: Data without action is just paperwork. Once B057 reveals the scale and patterns of waste, the cafe needs a strategy to address the root causes systematically. In UK cafes, the primary drivers of avoidable food waste are over-production (making more than is sold, particularly baked goods and prepared sandwiches), poor stock rotation (older stock pushed to the back of the fridge while newer stock is used first), inadequate demand forecasting (preparing the same quantities on a quiet Tuesday as a busy Saturday), and preparation errors (wrong orders, dropped items, failed batches). Each driver requires a different intervention. Over-production is addressed through production planning that adjusts quantities based on historical sales data and day-of-week patterns. Stock rotation is addressed through rigorous first-in-first-out (FIFO) discipline and clear date labelling. Demand forecasting improves through recording daily sales by item and using that data to predict future demand. Preparation errors reduce through training, standardised recipes (B050, B054), and quality checks. The financial opportunity is compelling. WRAP data suggests that for every £1 invested in food waste reduction, businesses see an average return of £14. A cafe that reduces its waste percentage from 20% to 10% on £3,000 monthly ingredient spend saves £300 per month — £3,600 per year — which flows directly to profit. This is often the single highest-impact intervention available to a cafe owner seeking to improve profitability without raising prices or increasing sales.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Review the waste data from B057 and identify the top five most-wasted items by value and volume. For each, determine the primary cause category. Implement the single most impactful quick win for each: if the top waste item is end-of-day unsold cake, reduce the morning bake quantity by 20-30% tomorrow and observe whether sales are affected (they usually are not — customers rarely buy the last slice anyway). If the top waste is expired milk, review ordering frequency and reduce order quantities. If the top waste is sink shots, invest in barista dial-in training (B039). Week 2: Implement FIFO stock rotation across all perishable storage. Mark every item entering the fridge or storeroom with its delivery date (use a marker pen or date stickers). Train all staff: older stock goes at the front, newer stock goes at the back. During prep, always use the oldest stock first. This single practice prevents a large proportion of expiry waste. Check all current stock for items past or close to their use-by date — use them today if safe, discard if not. Begin adjusting production quantities based on day-of-week demand patterns: if Monday consistently sells 30% fewer sandwiches than Friday, prepare 30% fewer on Monday.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Waste Reduction Action Plan documenting specific interventions for each major waste category. For over-production: create par level sheets for each prepared item by day of week, based on historical sales data. For expiry: implement a weekly stock check with date audit. For preparation errors: link to training from B050 and B054. For customer returns: analyse whether returns indicate a recipe or quality issue. Explore waste redistribution channels: register with Too Good To Go to sell surplus items at reduced prices rather than binning them (this generates revenue from waste and attracts new customers). Contact local food banks about donating suitable surplus. Allow staff to take home end-of-day items that would otherwise be wasted — this is both practical waste reduction and a staff benefit. Establish a monthly waste reduction target: start with a 5% reduction from the baseline, then set progressive targets each quarter. Calculate and publicise the financial savings achieved: 'Last month we saved £180 by reducing waste — that is equivalent to selling 60 extra flat whites.' Investigate composting for unavoidable food waste: coffee grounds (excellent compost material), fruit and vegetable peelings, and plate scraps. Partner with a local composting service or install a small food waste caddy for council food waste collection.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Waste reduction should now be embedded in daily decision-making. Production planning should be automatic and data-driven. Stock rotation should be a habit. The team should feel genuine ownership of waste targets. Pursue WRAP's hospitality sector targets: below 5% waste for the best-performing cafes. Introduce 'zero waste challenges' — weeks where the team attempts to eliminate avoidable waste entirely. Even if the target is not fully achieved, the mindset shift is valuable. Develop innovative uses for common waste items: coffee grounds for garden partnerships or body scrub products, banana skins for banana bread, stale bread for breadcrumbs or croutons. These transform waste into value and create an interesting story for customers. Track the cumulative financial savings from waste reduction since the programme began — this number grows impressively over months and years. Share the environmental impact: tonnes of CO2 saved, kilograms diverted from landfill. Use this data in marketing, sustainability reporting, and applications for environmental business awards.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Review B057 waste data and identify the top five most-wasted items by value
Implement one quick-win reduction action for each top five item
Implement FIFO stock rotation across all perishable storage
Mark all incoming stock with delivery dates
Adjust production quantities based on day-of-week demand patterns
Conduct a stock check clearing any expired items
Brief the team on the waste reduction objective and quick wins

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a Waste Reduction Action Plan with interventions for each cause category
Create par level sheets for prepared items adjusted by day of week
Register with Too Good To Go or similar platform for surplus sales
Establish a weekly stock check with date audit protocol
Set monthly waste reduction targets and track progress
Investigate composting and food waste collection options
Calculate and communicate financial savings from waste reduction

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Pursue WRAP's best-practice waste target (below 5%)
Introduce periodic 'zero waste challenges' for the team
Develop innovative uses for common waste materials
Track cumulative financial and environmental savings
Use waste reduction data in marketing and sustainability messaging
Apply for environmental business awards and certifications

deliverables: Par Level Sheets for prepared items (by day of week)
Surplus Redistribution Channel Guide (Too Good To Go, food banks, staff meals)

tools.description: Structured document listing the top waste items, root cause analysis for each, specific reduction interventions, responsible persons, target dates, and expected savings.
Production planning sheet showing the target preparation quantity for each item by day of week, based on historical sales data. Adjusted monthly as demand patterns shift seasonally.
Standard Operating Procedure for first-in-first-out stock rotation: date marking on receipt, storage positioning, prep usage sequence, and weekly date audit procedure.
Weekly inspection checklist for all perishable stock: verify date labels present, check use-by dates, identify items within 48 hours of expiry for priority use, and flag items for disposal.
Monthly report tracking waste percentage trend, progress against targets, financial savings achieved, redistribution volumes (Too Good To Go, donations), and environmental impact metrics.

failure_modes: Reduction targets are set without addressing root causes, creating pressure to hide waste rather than reduce it
FIFO is implemented but not maintained — during busy restocking, newer items end up at the front again
Par levels are set once and never adjusted for seasonal demand changes, school holidays, or weather patterns
Waste redistribution channels create administrative overhead without meaningful volume reduction
kpis: Waste percentage reduced by at least 5 percentage points within the first three months
FIFO stock rotation verified during 100% of weekly date audits
Par level adherence above 80% (prepared quantities within 20% of target)
Financial savings from waste reduction quantified and reported monthly
constraints: Waste reduction must not compromise food safety — never extend shelf lives or relax temperature controls to reduce waste
Too Good To Go and similar platforms require registration, item listing, and operational management
Par level accuracy depends on reliable sales data — invest in proper EPOS tracking

time_load.L1: 3 hours (data analysis, quick wins, FIFO implementation, team briefing)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (action plan, par levels, redistribution setup, stock audit system)

review_protocol: Weekly stock date audit. Weekly waste review meeting (from B057). Monthly waste percentage tracking against targets. Monthly par level accuracy check. Quarterly comprehensive waste strategy review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block turns waste data into action, implementing systematic reduction strategies targeting over-production, stock rotation, preparation errors, and demand forecasting. It covers par levels, FIFO rotation, waste redistribution channels, and measurable reduction targets.
implementation_risks: Aggressive waste reduction targets can lead to stock-outs that disappoint customers
Too Good To Go bags require end-of-day preparation and collection management
Staff may feel constrained by par levels and unable to respond to unexpected demand spikes
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate after B057 has produced at least 2 weeks of waste data. The strategy requires data to target interventions effectively. Flag if signals indicate high waste costs, frequent end-of-day discards, or poor stock rotation practices.
